cover.title: THE PIONEER
cover.subtitle: The one who lights the fire, and forgets to check who's moving with them.
cover.tagline: You light the fire and keep pulling forward. What you're learning now is how not to carry it alone.

profile_scores.caption: High drive and expression, low stability. You light the fire and keep pulling forward, but you don't know how to stop.

short_summary: You move fast and pull people into momentum before they even decide to follow, and you rarely stop to check if they're moving as much as you are.
The exhaustion you've felt wasn't from leading too much. It was from never once meeting someone who pulled alongside you.
What you're still learning isn't how to move first. It's how to trust that the relationship stays safe even when you're not the one pulling it forward.

full_report.headline: THE PIONEER
full_report.intro: You're not someone who agonizes before falling in love. If you're drawn to someone, you just move. You make the opening, approach first, see what happens. This report follows the pattern underneath that: why you keep becoming the one who pulls the relationship forward alone, what it's cost you, and what actually changes it.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You're not someone who agonizes before falling in love. If you're drawn to someone, you just move. You make the opening, approach first, see what happens.
People around you think you're confident: someone who does things without hesitation, someone who sets the tone. What's actually inside that, most don't know.

When you see possibility, you can't wait. You move first, build first. That's what makes you rare. It's also exactly why you get hurt, because sometimes you keep going without checking whether they're moving as much as you are.
chapters.profile_note: High Drive · High Expression, Low Stability. You light the fire and keep pulling forward, but you don't know how to stop.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Keep Repeating
body: You've done this before, haven't you? Probably more than once. You were drawn to someone. So you moved. Made plans, set the mood, proposed the next step. They just followed along.

Then one day you looked up, and you were the one deciding everything in this relationship. It's not that you disliked them. At some point it just became "a relationship that stops if I don't pull it," and there was no one to pull it for you.
chapters.highlight: That exhaustion you felt. It wasn't from leading too much. It was from never once meeting someone who pulled alongside you.

chart.title: THE EXHAUSTION CURVE
chart.description: You hold on through sheer drive as the relationship goes on, then suddenly stop, all at once.

chapters.title: What You've Gotten Wrong About Yourself

items.truth: You do hesitate. You just move before it finishes, so the gap stays invisible to everyone else. It's not speed. It's covering hesitation with action, until it starts to feel like personality. Just an old habit.
items.truth_short: You hesitate too. You just move before it finishes, so no one ever sees it.

items.belief: If I move first, they'll follow.
items.truth: The potential you saw wasn't fiction. You're fast at spotting it. But following and being swept along are different things. Bet too hard on what you saw, and they might not have agreed. They might've just been pulled in. People decide at their own pace, no matter how early you saw it.
items.truth_short: Following and being swept along aren't the same: people decide at their own pace.

items.belief: I got hurt because I approached first.
items.truth: No. Approaching first wasn't the problem. Pushing forward afterward without checking if they actually wanted it was. This isn't a "too fast" problem. It's a "skipped checking" problem, and the fix isn't slowing down. It's asking as you go.
items.truth_short: The issue wasn't speed. It was skipping the check in afterward.

items.belief: Deciding fast has never cost me anything.
items.truth: What you regret most isn't loving the wrong person. It's deciding too fast. You leave almost no gap between seeing potential and confirming it's real. So by the time you find out you were wrong, you're already in too deep.
items.truth_short: You suffered more from deciding too fast than from loving the wrong person.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: The face changes every time. The situation feels different every time. But look closely and the pattern is always the same. You're drawn to people who aren't sure yet: people who hesitate, who can't decide, who feel relieved seeing your certainty.
When someone follows you, it feels like love. It feels like a good fit, like you're leading them somewhere. But someone "following" you and someone "choosing" you are not the same thing.
chapters.highlight: Someone who follows you stops the moment you stop. Someone who chose you moves again on their own, even when you've stopped.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't someone who reacts to your momentum. You've already met enough of those.
What you actually need is someone who doesn't leave when you stop. Someone who proposes things on their own, without you pulling. Someone who makes you feel like you don't always have to lead.
chapters.action_sentence: In the right relationship, you're not the only one pulling.

chapters.title: Where You Go From Here
body: You already know how to approach someone. You've done it properly. More than once.

What you're still learning is how to trust that the relationship is safe even when you're not the one pulling. Without testing it. Without assuming it'll end if you stop pulling. Learning to just follow sometimes.
chapters.transition_note: This isn't some flaw in your character. It's just what naturally happens to someone who's led alone for too long.

chapters.title: The Types You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: The Person Who Hasn't Decided Yet
items.description: Someone who isn't sure what they want. But you see potential in them that even they haven't seen yet. So naturally, you set the direction for them. It takes a long time before you realize. You signed up to see their potential for them and decide on their behalf, not to be their partner.

items.type_title: The Person Who Only Reacts to Your Energy
items.description: They love your drive. They follow exactly as hard as you push. At first it feels like a great fit. But the moment you stop, they stop too. They never had drive of their own.

items.type_title: The Person Who Loves Your Certainty
items.description: They love watching you decide without hesitation. But loving your certainty and knowing what to do when you're unsteady are completely different things. This person needs you to stay certain. They have no idea what to do when you're not.

items.type_title: The Person Who Envies Your Drive
items.description: They want to be like you, watching you move without hesitation. But they themselves don't move. They just watch from the side while you do. It's vicarious, not going with you.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: These signs were always there. It was just always too natural for you to be the one deciding everything.

signal_strength.label: You decide almost everything. Plans, dates, what to talk about
If they just say "sounds good," you take that as proof it's a good match
The pace of the relationship, the next step, the direction. You decide all of it
They've almost never proposed anything first

chapters.core_signal: If you don't reach out first, they don't either. Create silence, and the answer is in what happens.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like for You

checklist.title: The relationship doesn't stop just because you're not pulling.
checklist.description: You don't need to lead. Sometimes you can just follow, and things keep moving fine.

checklist.title: They stay even when you hesitate.
checklist.description: You don't have to be certain all the time. Unsteady, indecisive, stalled. They stay anyway. Not pushing you, just staying close.

checklist.title: They're drawn to you, not your energy.
checklist.description: The right person doesn't leave when you stop. They're there because of you, not your momentum.

checklist.title: Deciding together makes you less lonely, not more.
checklist.description: You don't feel lonely after deciding alone. You feel lighter after deciding together. For you, that's not a small difference. It's everything.

chapters.title: What Happens In Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You can't stand standing still. You always need to be moving, deciding. So you're constantly moving forward, and you won't let anyone slow your pace. The type that doesn't work for you: someone who, like you, needs to lead everything themselves. Neither of you stops, so you collide, because you both want to be the one pulling.
sections.who_steadies_you: The surprisingly good fit: someone who follows when you lead, and doesn't disappear when you stop. With someone like this, you leading isn't one sided. It's just a good rhythm.
sections.same_pattern_repeats: You'll be drawn fast again next time. The moment you see potential, you'll move almost immediately. That won't change. That's just who you are.
sections.next_signs: Remember the signs from Chapter 8? They usually showed up months in. This time they'll appear sooner, and smaller. Whether they've actually done anything themselves toward the potential you saw in them. That shows up within the first few weeks.
sections.try_this:
Before, you invested the instant you saw potential. There was almost no gap between seeing it and being certain about it. This time, after seeing the potential, check one thing first: whether they're moving toward their own potential on their own. Are they moving because you're pulling them, or because they're genuinely moving themselves? Check that difference once, before you invest.

Normally you'd move first. This time, do the opposite. Just once, do nothing first. Don't make plans, don't reach out first. Just watch whether they move. Someone who's right for you will reach out or propose something within a few days. Someone who isn't will let the silence continue, waiting for you to break it first.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to move first. What's left is learning to wait for someone to move toward you too.
action_items: This week, wait once before making the next move, and see who steps forward on their own.
Before you invest in someone's potential, check whether they're moving toward it themselves, not just because you're pulling them.
When you notice the urge to fill a silence, sit with it for a few days instead and watch what happens.

closing.message: You've spent years learning how to move first. This report covered why you always end up being the one who approaches first, and how you relearn the art of stepping back.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
